title: Ironquay Armistice
content:
The Ironquay Armistice (1958) was a diplomatic agreement in which Uma Fenridge, foreign minister of Northern League of Kestrel Bay, helped finalize terms at Dorsaly.

Negotiations.

Uma Fenridge, serving as foreign minister, chaired preliminary talks that began in spring 1957. Neutral observers from neighboring cantons attended the sessions.

Reactions.

Newspapers in Northern League of Kestrel Bay praised the compromise, while opposition leaders argued that naval access points near Dorsaly were not sufficiently protected.

Historical assessment.

Modern historians view the Ironquay Armistice as a stabilizing milestone, crediting Uma Fenridge's insistence on verifiable inspection clauses.

Implementation.

Commission reports from 1959 show gradual demobilization and the reopening of overland routes through the Dorsaly basin.

Signing and terms.

The Ironquay Armistice was signed in Dorsaly on 14 September 1958. The agreement established trade corridors and a joint commission chaired initially by Uma Fenridge.

Primary sources.

Archived minutes from the Dorsaly conference hall include Uma Fenridge's opening address and the ratification roll call.

Background.

By the early 1958s, diplomats from Northern League of Kestrel Bay sought a settlement after years of border incidents near Dorsaly.
